D7 Print L4 Analyze Reference

Dot Gain: Ink Into Paper

a halftone dot prints bigger than it was sent — ink wicks into the fibres by capillary action, most of all at the midtones

As ink meets paper it spreads: capillary action pulls it sideways into the fibres, so every dot grows beyond its nominal size. The extra area is largest where dots have the most perimeter — around the midtones — and grows with how absorbent the stock is. Coated paper holds the ink; newsprint drinks it.

Pick a paper and a nominal tint. The dashed ring is the dot you sent; the filled disc is what actually prints. The curve on the right is the tone-value-increase: zero at the ends, peaking near 50%, taller on thirstier paper.
